Category: --- SUBSTRUCTURE: Below-grade and underground infrastructure — site prep, foundations, and buried utilities. ---

Description: Bulk and detailed excavation, sheeting, shoring, and underpinning. Typical trades: equipment operators, general laborers. Often the first major activity on site — schedule and cost overruns here cascade through the project.
Common Values: Mass excavation, trench excavation, sheet piling, soldier pile and lagging, soil nailing
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; HeavyBid
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs and cost codes exist but often lumped under general sitework
Benchmarking Use: Compare excavation cost per CY by soil type and project type
Notes: Cost codes often combine all sitework. Daily logs are the best signal for earthwork activities.

Description: Structural fill, fine grading, compaction, and subgrade preparation. Equipment-heavy — dozers, loaders, compactors. Quality depends on compaction testing (typically third-party geotechnical).
Common Values: Structural fill, gravel, crushed stone, compaction to 95% Proctor
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; HeavyBid
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs capture activity but rarely its own cost code
Benchmarking Use: Compare backfill cost per CY and compaction pass rates across soil conditions
Notes: Often combined with excavation in cost codes. Third-party test results live outside project management systems.

Description: Wellpoints, sump pumps, and groundwater control during construction. Triggered by water table conditions — not present on every project. Can become a major unplanned cost if unexpected.
Common Values: Wellpoint systems, sump pumps, deep wells
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — rarely its own cost code; shows up in daily logs and delay records
Benchmarking Use: Compare dewatering cost as % of substructure by region and soil type
Notes: Highly variable — some projects zero cost; others major budget item. Daily log delays (type: Existing Conditions) may capture water-related issues.

Description: Silt fence, construction entrances, inlet protection, and SWPPP compliance. Required by EPA/state permits — inspected regularly. Trades: general laborers.
Common Values: Silt fence, erosion blankets, inlet protection, construction entrance
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — safety/environmental observations and inspections capture violations
Benchmarking Use: Compare erosion control violation rates by region and project type
Notes: Often in general conditions cost code. Environmental observations and inspections are the strongest signal for compliance issues.

Description: Driven piles, drilled shafts, caissons, micropiles, and helical piers. Requires specialized subcontractors and equipment. Production rates (piles per day) are a key schedule driver. Trades: pile drivers, equipment operators.
Common Values: Steel H-piles, precast concrete piles, drilled shafts, micropiles, auger cast piles
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; HeavyBid; PileNavigator
Procore Coverage: Medium — dedicated cost codes on heavy projects; daily logs track production
Benchmarking Use: Compare pile driving production rates and cost per LF by pile type
Notes: Daily log productivity records may capture pile counts. RFIs for pile capacity and obstructions are common.

Description: Spread footings, strip footings, mat foundations, and pier pads. Concrete and formwork are the primary cost drivers. Trades: concrete workers, carpenters (formwork). Pour records track volume and placement.
Common Values: Spread footings, strip footings, mat slab, pier pads
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — under general concrete code; daily logs capture pour activities
Benchmarking Use: Compare footing concrete cost per CY and pour cycle times by project type
Notes: Often grouped under general concrete cost code. Hard to separate from other concrete elements.

Description: Cast-in-place or precast foundation walls and retaining walls. Formwork complexity drives cost. Waterproofing is a critical follow-on trade. Trades: concrete workers, carpenters.
Common Values: CIP walls, precast foundation walls, retaining walls
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — usually under same code as footings
Benchmarking Use: Compare foundation wall cost per SF of wall face by height and forming method
Notes: Rarely separated from footings in cost data. Daily log notes for wall pours help.

Description: Reinforced concrete beams connecting foundation elements at or below grade. Transfer loads between piles/piers. Trades: concrete workers, carpenters.
Common Values: Grade beams, tie beams
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — lumped with other foundation concrete
Benchmarking Use: Compare grade beam cost per LF by beam size
Notes: Rarely its own cost code. RFIs for reinforcing conflicts are common.

Description: Ground-supported concrete floor slabs with vapor barriers and joint sealants. Includes reinforcement (rebar, WWF, fiber). Quality depends on subgrade prep and finishing. Trades: concrete workers.
Common Values: 4" SOG, 6" SOG, post-tensioned SOG, fiber-reinforced, vapor barrier
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — sometimes its own code; daily logs capture pour records
Benchmarking Use: Compare slab cost per SF by thickness and reinforcement type
Notes: Daily log pour records are a good signal. Observations capture flatness and cracking issues.

Description: Foundation waterproofing membranes, dampproofing, underslab vapor barriers, and drainage boards. Failure leads to long-term moisture intrusion. Trades: specialty waterproofing applicators.
Common Values: Fluid-applied membrane, sheet membrane, bentonite, drainage board, dampproofing
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — often its own code on larger projects; quality observations capture failures
Benchmarking Use: Compare waterproofing warranty claim rates and cost per SF by membrane type
Notes: Quality observations are a strong signal — waterproofing failures are a top rework driver.

Description: Underground domestic and fire water supply piping from street to building. Requires coordination with municipality. Trades: plumbers/pipefitters, equipment operators.
Common Values: Ductile iron pipe, copper service, valves, meters
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — usually grouped under site utilities
Benchmarking Use: Compare site utility cost per LF of trench by soil conditions
Notes: Inspection records help when specific utility inspections exist. Municipality coordination adds schedule risk.

Description: Underground sanitary waste piping from building to main, with manholes and cleanouts. Gravity-driven — slope is critical. Trades: plumbers/pipefitters, equipment operators.
Common Values: PVC pipe, concrete manholes, cleanouts, fittings
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — usually grouped under site utilities
Benchmarking Use: Compare sanitary sewer cost per LF by pipe diameter
Notes: Similar to water — often grouped in cost codes. Daily logs capture installation activities.

Description: Underground storm water piping, catch basins, and retention/detention systems. Sized by hydrological analysis. Subject to local stormwater regulations. Trades: plumbers/pipefitters, equipment operators.
Common Values: HDPE/PVC pipe, concrete structures, catch basins, detention vaults
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — usually grouped under site utilities
Benchmarking Use: Compare storm drainage cost per acre of impervious area
Notes: Sometimes combined with sanitary in cost codes. Detention/retention system costs vary significantly by locality.

Description: Underground gas piping from meter to building. Small scope on most projects but requires specialty inspection. Trades: plumbers/pipefitters.
Common Values: Steel/PE gas pipe, fittings, valves, meter
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — often too small for its own cost code
Benchmarking Use: Compare gas service cost per project type
Notes: Inspection records for gas testing are a distinct signal when present.

Description: Underground electrical and telecom conduit banks, manholes, and handholes. Coordinates with power company. Trades: electricians, equipment operators.
Common Values: PVC conduit, concrete encasement, handholes, manholes
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — usually grouped under site electrical
Benchmarking Use: Compare ductbank cost per LF by conduit count
Notes: Less field data visibility than building MEP. Often utility company coordination delays.

Category: --- SUPERSTRUCTURE: Structural frame and primary load-bearing systems above grade. ---

Description: Steel or concrete vertical load-bearing members. Steel columns: W-shapes, HSS, pipe. Concrete columns: CIP with rebar cages, formwork. Trades: ironworkers (steel), concrete workers (concrete). Critical path activity.
Common Values: W-shapes, HSS, CIP concrete, rebar cages
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Tekla; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — under general structural code; RFIs help differentiate elements
Benchmarking Use: Compare structural steel cost per ton or concrete cost per CY by building type
Notes: Cost codes usually cover all structural concrete or all structural steel — not individual elements. RFI topics help differentiate.

Description: Steel or concrete horizontal spanning members. Steel: W-shapes, built-up sections. Concrete: CIP or precast. Camber and deflection are key design parameters. Trades: ironworkers, concrete workers.
Common Values: W-shapes, built-up sections, precast beams, CIP beams
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Tekla; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — same structural code as columns; RFIs and observations provide element detail
Benchmarking Use: Compare beam deflection RFI rates by span length
Notes: RFIs and observations provide element-level detail that cost codes don't separate.

Description: Composite metal deck with concrete topping, post-tensioned slabs, or precast plank. Deck erection and concrete pours are major scheduling milestones. Trades: ironworkers (deck), concrete workers (topping).
Common Values: 1.5" and 3" composite deck, lightweight concrete topping, PT slabs, precast hollow core
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Tekla; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily logs capture pour records well
Benchmarking Use: Compare concrete topping cost per SF and pour cycle times
Notes: Concrete pour records in daily logs are a strong signal. Deck erection tracked separately on some projects.

Description: Steel or concrete roof framing — long-span joists, trusses, and moment frames. Trades: ironworkers. Often governs crane duration and pick sequence.
Common Values: Open-web steel joists, trusses, moment frames, long-span beams
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Tekla; Revit
Procore Coverage: Low — not easily separated from other structural steel
Benchmarking Use: Compare roof structure weight per SF by span and occupancy
Notes: Usually under structural steel code. Joist and truss deliveries are scheduling milestones.

Description: Bolted and welded connections, embed plates, anchor bolts, and base plates. Connection design generates heavy RFI activity — a leading indicator of structural complexity. Trades: ironworkers.
Common Values: High-strength bolts, weld rod, embed plates, base plates, shear studs
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Tekla; SDS/2
Procore Coverage: Medium — RFIs are the strongest signal for connection complexity
Benchmarking Use: Compare structural connection RFI count per ton of steel
Notes: RFIs are the strongest signal — structural connections generate heavy RFI activity.

Description: Cast-in-place or steel stairs and structural ramps, with handrails. Both temporary and permanent stairs. Punch items frequently reference stairs and railings. Trades: ironworkers, concrete workers.
Common Values: Steel pan stairs, precast stairs, CIP stairs, structural ramps
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — punch items commonly reference stairs
Benchmarking Use: Compare stair defect rate per floor count
Notes: Often under misc metals or concrete code. Punch items are a good signal for stairs.

Description: Lintels, loose angles, shelf angles, channels, bollards, and structural railings. Small scope individually but high coordination — nearly every other trade needs miscellaneous metals embedded. Trades: ironworkers.
Common Values: Lintels, shelf angles, channels, bollards, railings, embed plates
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Tekla
Procore Coverage: Medium — often has its own cost code; RFIs and observations are good signals
Benchmarking Use: Compare misc metals cost as % of structural steel by building type
Notes: High coordination burden — lintels and shelf angles interact with masonry; embeds interact with concrete.

Category: --- EXTERIOR CLOSURE: Building envelope — walls, windows, roofing. Performance-critical for weather, thermal, and moisture control. ---

Description: Aluminum-framed glass curtain wall systems — unitized or stick-built. High RFI and coordination issue activity (MEP penetrations, structural anchors). Major submittal packages. Trades: glaziers, curtain wall installers.
Common Values: Unitized curtain wall, stick-built, structural glazing, spider systems
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam; Revit
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated cost code; strong RFI and observation signals
Benchmarking Use: Compare curtain wall cost per SF of glass area by system type
Notes: Coordination issues are common — MEP penetrations through curtain wall require early modeling.

Description: Punched window units in opaque wall systems. Product submittals drive procurement timeline. Trades: glaziers.
Common Values: Aluminum, vinyl, wood-clad windows; fixed, operable, awning, casement
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — sometimes combined with curtain wall or doors in cost codes
Benchmarking Use: Compare window cost per unit by type and performance rating
Notes: Submittals are a strong signal — specific product approvals and lead times.

Description: Entrance doors, storefronts, revolving doors, overhead doors, and service doors. Door hardware drives complexity. Trades: glaziers (storefronts), carpenters (hollow metal). Doors are one of the highest punch item categories.
Common Values: Storefront entrances, revolving doors, hollow metal, overhead coiling, sliding glass
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — doors are a top punch item category with strong cost code presence
Benchmarking Use: Compare door hardware cost per door by building type
Notes: Doors are one of the highest punch item categories across all project types.

Description: Brick veneer, stone veneer, and CMU backup walls with through-wall flashing and weep systems. Quality depends on mortar joints, flashing, and drainage. Trades: masons.
Common Values: Brick veneer, natural stone, manufactured stone, CMU backup, cavity wall
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — usually its own cost code; quality observations for masonry are common
Benchmarking Use: Compare masonry cost per SF by material type and region
Notes: Quality observations for mortar joints and flashing failures are common signals.

Description: Architectural precast concrete, insulated metal panels (IMP), ACM panels, and terra cotta. Panel layout coordination with structure and MEP is critical. Trades: ironworkers (erection), specialty installers.
Common Values: Architectural precast, insulated metal panels, ACM, fiber cement, terra cotta
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — usually its own cost code; coordination issues common for panel layout
Benchmarking Use: Compare cladding cost per SF by panel type
Notes: Coordination issues for panel layout vs. MEP penetrations and structural embed locations.

Description: Fluid-applied or sheet membrane air/vapor barriers on exterior sheathing. Performance-critical — failures here cause long-term moisture damage. Third-party inspection is common. Trades: specialty applicators.
Common Values: Fluid-applied membrane, self-adhered sheet, spray-applied, primers
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — observations and inspections are the strongest signal
Benchmarking Use: Compare air barrier defect rate per SF of wall area
Notes: Critical quality item. Observation and inspection records are the strongest signal for failures.

Description: Continuous insulation (CI) on exterior walls — mineral wool, rigid foam, or spray foam. Energy code compliance driver. Trades: specialty installers, general laborers.
Common Values: Rigid polyiso, mineral wool, EPS, XPS, spray foam
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — often combined with air barrier or cladding in cost codes
Benchmarking Use: Compare CI R-value and cost per SF by climate zone
Notes: Energy code compliance increasingly requires continuous insulation. Often bundled with air barrier work.

Description: Single-ply (TPO, PVC, EPDM), built-up, or modified bitumen roofing membranes. Warranty is a key deliverable — manufacturer requirements drive installation quality. Trades: roofers.
Common Values: TPO, PVC, EPDM, modified bitumen, built-up roofing
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — almost always its own cost code; strong observation and punch item activity
Benchmarking Use: Compare roofing cost per square and warranty claim rates by membrane type
Notes: Strong signal across observations and punch items. Submittals specify exact membrane product and warranty requirements.

Description: Rigid insulation and tapered systems for drainage, with cover boards. Tapered layout is a design coordination item. Trades: roofers.
Common Values: Polyiso, EPS, XPS, gypsum cover board, tapered crickets
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — often included in roofing cost code
Benchmarking Use: Compare roof insulation R-value per climate zone
Notes: RFIs for roof assembly details and drainage design.

Description: Roof edge metal, copings, counterflashing, reglets, gutters, and downspouts. Flashing failures are a top source of roof leaks. Trades: roofers, sheet metal workers.
Common Values: Aluminum copings, galvanized gutters, copper flashing, stainless counterflashing
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — observations for improper flashing are common
Benchmarking Use: Compare flashing-related leak rates by material and roof type
Notes: Observations for flashing defects are a common quality signal. Sometimes its own code; sometimes under roofing.

Description: Interior roof drains, scuppers, overflow drains, and conductor piping. Cross-trade coordination — roofer installs drain bodies, plumber runs pipe. Design drives structural loads (ponding).
Common Values: Interior roof drains, scuppers, overflow drains, conductor pipe
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — coordination issues for drain-to-structure conflicts are a signal
Benchmarking Use: Compare roof drainage density (drains per SF) by roof area and local code
Notes: Cross-trade coordination between roofer and plumber. Coordination issues are the best signal.

Description: Unit skylights, smoke vents, and roof access hatches. Small scope but high defect rate per unit — curb flashing is a common failure point. Trades: glaziers, specialty installers.
Common Values: Unit skylights, tubular skylights, smoke vents, roof hatches
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — small scope; punch items capture defects
Benchmarking Use: Compare skylight leak rate and cost per unit
Notes: High defect rate per unit. Curb flashing around skylights is a frequent quality issue.

Category: --- MEP SYSTEMS: Mechanical, electrical, plumbing, fire protection, and low-voltage technology. Highest coordination complexity. ---

Description: Supply, return, and exhaust ductwork — sheet metal, flex, and fittings. Routing coordination with other MEP systems drives schedule. Trades: HVAC/sheet metal workers.
Common Values: Sheet metal rectangular, round/spiral, flex duct, lined duct, fittings
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit; Navisworks
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated cost codes; strong signals across observations; punch items; RFIs; and coordination issues
Benchmarking Use: Compare ductwork cost per CFM by system type and building occupancy
Notes: Strong signals across multiple field tools. Coordination issues between mechanical, electrical, and plumbing are a major schedule driver.

Description: Chilled water, hot water, condenser water, and refrigerant piping with insulation. Pipe sizing and routing are key RFI topics. Trades: pipefitters.
Common Values: Steel, copper, CPVC pipe; chilled water, hot water, condenser water, refrigerant
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — often under general HVAC code
Benchmarking Use: Compare HVAC piping cost per ton of cooling capacity
Notes: Observation and RFI signals help differentiate piping from general HVAC work.

Description: AHUs, RTUs, fan coil units, VAV boxes, and exhaust fans. Major submittals — equipment delivery is a key scheduling milestone. Trades: HVAC mechanics.
Common Values: Rooftop units, AHUs, fan coil units, VAV boxes, exhaust fans
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit
Procore Coverage: High — major submittals; equipment setting is a schedule milestone
Benchmarking Use: Compare AHU cost per CFM and lead time by equipment type
Notes: Coordination issues for equipment clearances are common. Long lead items — procurement timing is critical.

Description: Boilers, unit heaters, radiant heating, and heat exchangers. Seasonal dependency — heating system must be operational for winter work. Trades: HVAC mechanics.
Common Values: Boilers (gas/electric), unit heaters, radiant panels, heat exchangers
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — sometimes under general HVAC code
Benchmarking Use: Compare heating cost per BTU capacity by fuel type and climate zone
Notes: Temporary heat is often needed before permanent heating is operational — tracked in daily logs.

Description: Chillers, cooling towers, condensing units, and split systems. Heaviest and most expensive MEP equipment — rigging and crane logistics are critical. Trades: HVAC mechanics.
Common Values: Centrifugal chillers, air-cooled chillers, cooling towers, condensing units, VRF
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals and delivery logs are strong signals
Benchmarking Use: Compare cooling cost per ton of capacity by equipment type
Notes: Major equipment — delivery logistics and rigging are schedule drivers.

Description: Thermal and acoustic insulation on ductwork. Required by energy code on exposed supply and return duct. Trades: insulators.
Common Values: Fiberglass wrap, elastomeric, foil-faced rigid, duct liner
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — usually under general HVAC code
Benchmarking Use: Compare insulation cost per SF of duct surface
Notes: Punch items flag missing or damaged insulation. Often bundled with general HVAC code.

Description: DDC controls, thermostats, sensors, damper actuators, and building automation system (BAS) programming. Controls/commissioning generates heavy RFI and punch item activity. Trades: controls technicians, HVAC mechanics.
Common Values: DDC controllers, thermostats, CO2 sensors, damper actuators, BAS software
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Tridium; Honeywell
Procore Coverage: Medium — often its own cost code; RFIs and punch items are strong signals
Benchmarking Use: Compare controls cost as % of mechanical and commissioning defect rates
Notes: Controls sequences of operation generate heavy RFI activity. Late-project commissioning drives punch items.

Description: Air and water system balancing and commissioning verification. Late-project activity — results validate design assumptions. Trades: TAB contractors, commissioning agents.
Common Values: Air balancing, water balancing, sound testing, vibration testing
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; TAB reports
Procore Coverage: Low — inspection and punch item records capture commissioning issues
Benchmarking Use: Compare TAB deficiency rates and commissioning duration by building type
Notes: Punch items and commissioning inspections are primary signals. Usually late-project activity.

Description: Hot and cold water distribution piping, valves, hangers, and insulation. Leak testing is a key milestone. Trades: plumbers/pipefitters.
Common Values: Copper, PEX, CPVC, stainless steel pipe; valves, hangers, insulation
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — under general plumbing code; leak observations are a strong signal
Benchmarking Use: Compare domestic water cost per fixture count
Notes: Observations for leaks are a strong signal. Pipe material choices vary by region and code.

Description: DWV piping, cleanouts, floor drains, and traps. Gravity-driven — slope is critical and generates RFIs for routing in tight ceilings. Trades: plumbers/pipefitters.
Common Values: Cast iron, PVC, ABS pipe; cleanouts, floor drains, P-traps
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — under general plumbing code
Benchmarking Use: Compare DWV cost per fixture count by pipe material
Notes: RFIs for routing and slope are common. Cast iron vs. PVC is a cost and noise trade-off.

Description: Interior roof drain leaders, area drains, and conductor piping. Connects roof drainage to underground storm system. Trades: plumbers/pipefitters.
Common Values: Cast iron, PVC conductor pipe; roof drains, area drains
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — often combined with sanitary
Benchmarking Use: Compare interior storm drainage cost per roof drain count
Notes: Small scope. Often combined with sanitary plumbing in cost codes.

Description: Interior natural gas distribution to equipment — boilers, water heaters, kitchen equipment. Requires pressure testing and inspection. Trades: plumbers/pipefitters.
Common Values: Black steel pipe, fittings, valves, flex connectors, regulators
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — small scope; inspection records for gas testing are a distinct signal
Benchmarking Use: Compare gas piping cost per equipment connection
Notes: Gas inspections are a distinct signal. Small scope on most projects.

Description: Toilets, urinals, sinks, faucets, drinking fountains, and floor drains. High submittal activity (product approvals) and very high punch item rates — plumbing fixtures are a top punch category. Trades: plumbers.
Common Values: Water closets, urinals, lavatories, kitchen sinks, drinking fountains, mop sinks
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — strong submittal and punch item signals; dedicated cost codes common
Benchmarking Use: Compare fixture cost per unit and punch item rate per fixture by building type
Notes: Very high punch item rate — plumbing fixtures are a top punch category across all project types.

Description: Domestic water heating — tanks, tankless, and heat pump water heaters. Small scope but affects hot water distribution design. Trades: plumbers.
Common Values: Tank water heaters, tankless, heat pump, solar preheat
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — submittal for specific unit
Benchmarking Use: Compare water heater cost per gallon of storage by fuel type
Notes: Small scope. Submittal for specific unit selection.

Description: Thermal insulation on plumbing piping. Required by energy code on hot water and chilled supply lines. Trades: insulators.
Common Values: Fiberglass, elastomeric, phenolic pipe insulation
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — usually under general plumbing code
Benchmarking Use: Compare pipe insulation cost per LF by pipe size
Notes: Punch items flag missing insulation. Often bundled with general plumbing.

Description: Main switchgear, distribution panels, transformers, bus duct, and feeders. Major submittals with long lead times — procurement timing is critical. Trades: electricians.
Common Values: Switchgear, distribution panels, transformers, bus duct, feeders, metering
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated cost codes; strong submittal and RFI signals
Benchmarking Use: Compare electrical distribution cost per connected kVA by building type
Notes: Major submittals with long lead times. RFIs for panel sizing and circuit loading are common.

Description: Outlets, switches, junction boxes, home runs, and branch circuits. Highest-volume electrical work. Punch items for cover plates and device alignment are top closeout issues. Trades: electricians.
Common Values: Wire (12 AWG, 10 AWG), boxes, receptacles, switches, cover plates, connectors
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — strong punch item signal; dedicated cost codes
Benchmarking Use: Compare outlet/switch count per SF and device defect rate
Notes: Cover plates and device alignment are the highest-volume electrical punch items.

Description: EMT, rigid, PVC conduit, cable tray, and wireways. Routing coordination with other MEP systems is a major schedule driver. Trades: electricians.
Common Values: EMT, rigid conduit, PVC conduit, cable tray, wireways, fittings
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — usually under general electrical code; coordination issues for routing are common
Benchmarking Use: Compare conduit cost per LF by type and installation method
Notes: Coordination issues for conduit routing are common — especially in tight ceiling spaces.

Description: Interior and exterior lighting fixtures, controls, occupancy sensors, and daylight harvesting systems. High submittal and punch item activity. Trades: electricians.
Common Values: LED fixtures, controls, occupancy sensors, daylight sensors, dimming systems
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; AGi32; Revit
Procore Coverage: High — strong submittal and punch item signals; dedicated cost codes
Benchmarking Use: Compare lighting cost per SF and fixture density by occupancy type
Notes: Lighting is a high-volume punch category. Submittal packages for fixture selections drive procurement schedule.

Description: Emergency generators, automatic transfer switches (ATS), UPS systems, and emergency circuits. Code-required for life safety. Trades: electricians.
Common Values: Diesel/gas generators, ATS, UPS, fuel tanks, emergency panels
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — usually its own cost code; inspection records for generator testing
Benchmarking Use: Compare generator cost per kW and testing pass rates
Notes: Code-required testing and inspection records are a distinct signal.

Description: Building grounding system, lightning rods, and bonding. Code-required; tested during construction. Small scope. Trades: electricians.
Common Values: Copper ground wire, ground rods, lightning rods, bonding clamps
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — small scope; inspection records for grounding tests
Benchmarking Use: Compare grounding system cost as % of electrical by building height
Notes: Inspection records for grounding resistance tests are the primary signal.

Description: Fire sprinkler distribution piping — mains, cross-mains, and branch lines with seismic bracing. Head layout coordination with ceilings and MEP is a top coordination issue. Trades: sprinkler fitters.
Common Values: Black steel pipe, CPVC, fittings, hangers, seismic bracing
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Revit; Navisworks
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated cost codes; strong coordination issue signal
Benchmarking Use: Compare sprinkler system cost per SF and coordination issue rates
Notes: Sprinkler head layout vs. ceiling layout vs. other MEP is a top coordination challenge.

Description: Pendant, upright, sidewall, and concealed sprinkler heads. Very high punch item rate — concealed head cover plates are a top closeout item. Trades: sprinkler fitters.
Common Values: Pendant, upright, sidewall, concealed, ESFR heads; escutcheons
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — very high punch item rate; under fire protection code
Benchmarking Use: Compare sprinkler head defect rate per head count
Notes: Concealed head cover plates are consistently a top closeout punch item.

Description: Standpipe risers, hose valves, and fire department connections (FDC). Required for buildings above certain heights. Flow testing is a code requirement. Trades: sprinkler fitters.
Common Values: Steel pipe, hose valves, FDC assemblies, check valves
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — under general fire protection code; inspections for flow tests
Benchmarking Use: Compare standpipe cost per floor
Notes: Inspections for flow tests are a distinct signal.

Description: Fire pump, jockey pump, controller, and test header. Required when municipal water pressure is insufficient. Acceptance testing is code-required. Trades: sprinkler fitters, electricians.
Common Values: Electric/diesel fire pump, jockey pump, controller, test header
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — submittal signal; inspection records for acceptance testing
Benchmarking Use: Compare fire pump cost per GPM capacity
Notes: Distinct equipment — submittal and acceptance testing signals. Not present on all projects.

Description: Fire alarm control panel (FACP), pull stations, smoke/heat detectors, notification appliances, and duct detectors. Code-required system with acceptance testing. Trades: electricians, low-voltage technicians.
Common Values: FACP, smoke detectors, heat detectors, pull stations, horn/strobes, duct detectors
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated cost codes; strong punch item signal; acceptance testing inspections
Benchmarking Use: Compare fire alarm cost per device count and testing pass rates
Notes: Device alignment and labeling are common punch items. Acceptance testing inspections are a distinct signal. Usually its own cost code.

Description: Cat6/fiber optic cabling, patch panels, racks, and telecom rooms. Pathway coordination with electrical. Trades: low-voltage technicians.
Common Values: Cat6/6A, single-mode fiber, multi-mode fiber, patch panels, racks
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — sometimes its own code; punch items for labeling and testing
Benchmarking Use: Compare cabling cost per drop and data drop density per SF
Notes: Punch items for labeling and testing. Sometimes bundled with general electrical.

Description: CCTV cameras, access control (card readers, controllers), and intrusion detection. Integrates with door hardware — coordination signal. Trades: low-voltage technicians.
Common Values: IP cameras, card readers, controllers, door strikes, magnetic locks
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Lenel; Genetec
Procore Coverage: Medium — coordination signal for door hardware integration; submittals for system design
Benchmarking Use: Compare security system cost per controlled opening and camera density
Notes: Access control integrates with door hardware (Division 08) — cross-trade coordination.

Description: AV systems — displays, speakers, distributed audio, and conference room systems. Often owner-furnished / contractor-installed (OFCI). Trades: low-voltage technicians.
Common Values: Displays, projectors, speakers, amplifiers, control systems, cabling
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — submittals and punch items; often OFCI
Benchmarking Use: Compare AV cost per room and AV room density by building type
Notes: Often owner-furnished / contractor-installed — procurement responsibility varies.

Description: BAS field devices, sensors, actuators, and control wiring (not programming — see HVAC Controls). Different installer than the controls programmer. Trades: low-voltage technicians.
Common Values: Temperature sensors, humidity sensors, actuators, controllers, control wiring
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Tridium
Procore Coverage: Low — overlaps with HVAC controls in cost codes
Benchmarking Use: Compare BAS device count per SF and commissioning defect rates
Notes: BAS programming vs. device installation are usually different subcontractors. Overlaps with HVAC Controls.

Description: Nurse call, intercom, paging, distributed antenna (DAS), and clock systems. Project-type dependent — nurse call for healthcare, DAS for large buildings. Trades: low-voltage technicians.
Common Values: Nurse call, intercom, paging, DAS, master clock, mass notification
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — small scope; submittals and punch items
Benchmarking Use: Compare specialty system cost by system type and building occupancy
Notes: Project-type dependent. DAS increasingly required by fire code in large buildings.

Category: --- INTERIOR CONSTRUCTION: Partitions, finishes, fixtures, and specialties. Highest punch item density. ---

Description: Interior metal stud partitions, soffits, and bulkhead framing with blocking. Layout accuracy drives downstream finish quality. Trades: drywall hangers.
Common Values: 25-ga, 20-ga studs; 2.5", 3.625", 6" widths; deflection track, blocking
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — usually combined with drywall in cost codes; daily log and observation signals
Benchmarking Use: Compare framing cost per SF of partition by STC rating
Notes: Usually combined with drywall in cost codes. Blocking for wall-mounted items is a common coordination issue.

Description: Gypsum board installation, taping, and finishing — including specialty boards (moisture-resistant, fire-rated, abuse-resistant). Drywall damage and finish quality are top punch categories. Trades: drywall hangers, finishers.
Common Values: Type X, moisture-resistant, abuse-resistant, shaft liner; Level 3/4/5 finish
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — identifiable cost codes; strong observation and punch item signals
Benchmarking Use: Compare drywall cost per SF and defect rates by finish level
Notes: Strong observation and punch item signal. Drywall damage and finish quality are top punch categories.

Description: Interior hollow metal and wood doors, frames, and finish hardware (hinges, closers, locks, pulls). Hardware sets are complex submittals. Doors/hardware is consistently a top 3 punch item category. Trades: carpenters.
Common Values: Hollow metal, wood, FRP doors; hollow metal, aluminum frames; mortise, cylindrical locksets; closers, hinges, pulls
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — top 3 punch item category across all project types; detailed hardware submittals
Benchmarking Use: Compare door hardware cost per door and hardware defect rate by building type
Notes: Consistently one of the top 3 punch item categories across all project types.

Description: Suspended acoustic ceiling grid and tile systems. Layout coordination with lighting, sprinklers, and diffusers. Trades: ceiling installers.
Common Values: 2x2, 2x4 tegular/lay-in tile; exposed grid, concealed grid; specialty panels
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — common punch item category; usually has cost code
Benchmarking Use: Compare ceiling tile cost per SF and defect rate by tile type
Notes: Common punch item category — damaged tiles and grid alignment. RFIs for ceiling heights and clearances.

Description: Drywall ceilings, soffits, bulkheads, and light coves — non-acoustic applications. More complex framing than walls. Trades: drywall hangers, finishers.
Common Values: Flat ceilings, soffits, bulkheads, light coves, radius ceilings
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — lumped with general drywall; RFIs for dimensions
Benchmarking Use: Compare soffit/bulkhead cost per LF
Notes: RFIs for dimensions and detailing. Lumped with general drywall in cost codes.

Description: Interior painting, primer, specialty coatings, stain, and clear finishes. Paint/finish is the #1 punch item category universally — touch-ups and color inconsistency dominate closeout. Trades: painters.
Common Values: Latex paint, epoxy, urethane, stain, primer; Levels 1-5
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — #1 punch item category universally; almost always its own cost code
Benchmarking Use: Compare paint cost per SF and touch-up punch item rate by building type
Notes: Paint/finish is the #1 punch item category universally. Extremely strong signal.

Description: Vinyl wallcovering, specialty wall finishes, and wall protection (corner guards, crash rails). Common in healthcare and hospitality. Trades: painters, specialty installers.
Common Values: Vinyl wallcovering, fabric wallcovering, corner guards, crash rails, chair rails
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — sometimes under paint code; punch items for seams and adhesion
Benchmarking Use: Compare wallcovering cost per SF and defect rate by material type
Notes: Punch items for seams and adhesion failures. Project-type dependent.

Description: Ceramic, porcelain, and natural stone tile for floors and walls — including setting materials and grout. Cracked tiles, grout, and lippage are common punch items. Trades: tile setters.
Common Values: Ceramic, porcelain, marble, granite, limestone; thinset, grout, waterproofing
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — strong punch item signal; dedicated cost codes; submittals specify exact product
Benchmarking Use: Compare tile cost per SF and defect rate by material type
Notes: Strong punch item signal — cracked tiles and grout issues. Submittals specify exact tile product.

Description: Carpet (broadloom, tile), VCT, LVT, rubber, and sheet vinyl with resilient base. Seams, transitions, and base are frequent closeout items. Trades: floor installers.
Common Values: Carpet tile, broadloom, LVT, VCT, rubber, sheet vinyl, resilient base
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — common punch item category; dedicated cost codes
Benchmarking Use: Compare flooring cost per SF by material type and defect rates
Notes: Common punch item category. Seams and transitions are frequent closeout items.

Description: Polished concrete, epoxy, terrazzo, and resinous flooring. Project-type dependent — common in industrial, healthcare, and education. Trades: specialty installers.
Common Values: Polished concrete, epoxy, terrazzo, urethane, MMA
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — usually its own code when present; submittals for system specs
Benchmarking Use: Compare specialty flooring cost per SF by system type
Notes: Project-type dependent. Surface prep is critical — failures trace to poor subfloor preparation.

Description: Cabinets, countertops, shelving, built-in furniture, and wood trim (base/crown molding). High punch item rate — scratches, chips, alignment, and door adjustment. Trades: carpenters.
Common Values: Plastic laminate casework, wood veneer, solid surface counters, stone counters, wood trim
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — strong punch item signal; dedicated cost codes
Benchmarking Use: Compare casework cost per LF and defect rate by material type
Notes: High punch item rate — scratches, chips, alignment, and door adjustment are common closeout items.

Description: Solid surface, stone, quartz, and plastic laminate countertops. Templated after casework installation — template-to-install cycle is a schedule constraint. Trades: specialty installers.
Common Values: Quartz, granite, marble, solid surface, plastic laminate
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — often under casework code; submittals specify exact product
Benchmarking Use: Compare countertop cost per LF by material type
Notes: Submittals specify exact product and color. Template-to-fabrication lead time.

Description: Toilet partitions, accessories (paper holders, grab bars, soap dispensers), mirrors, and signage. High punch item rate — installation alignment, missing items, and damage. Trades: carpenters, general laborers.
Common Values: Toilet partitions, grab bars, paper holders, soap dispensers, mirrors, dryers
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: High — strong punch item signal; dedicated cost codes
Benchmarking Use: Compare accessory defect rate per restroom count
Notes: High punch item rate — installation alignment and missing items are common closeout issues.

Description: Room signs, directional signs, code-required signs, and ADA signage. Late-project scope — often last items installed before occupancy. Trades: specialty installers.
Common Values: Room ID signs, directional, code-required exit signs, ADA tactile signs
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — punch items for incorrect or missing signs; submittals for sign schedule
Benchmarking Use: Compare signage cost per sign and defect rate
Notes: Usually late project. Punch items for incorrect or missing signs. ADA compliance is key.

Category: --- CONVEYING SYSTEMS: Elevators and vertical transportation. ---

Description: Passenger, service, and freight elevator cars — hoistway doors, cab finishes, and controls. Elevator installation is a major scheduling milestone. Long lead time item. Trades: elevator mechanics.
Common Values: Traction passenger, hydraulic, MRL, service, freight elevators
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; Otis/Schindler/KONE/TK
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated cost codes; strong submittal signal; major schedule milestone
Benchmarking Use: Compare elevator cost per floor served and installation duration
Notes: Elevator installation is a major scheduling milestone. Punch items for cab finishes and door operation.

Description: Traction machines, hydraulic units, motor controllers, and dispatching systems. Acceptance testing/inspection by authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) is required. Trades: elevator mechanics.
Common Values: Gearless traction, geared traction, hydraulic power unit, MRL drives, controllers
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — under general elevator code; acceptance testing inspections
Benchmarking Use: Compare elevator machinery maintenance cost by type
Notes: Acceptance testing by AHJ is required before occupancy. Inspection records are a distinct signal.

Category: --- SITEWORK: Exterior site improvements and hardscape. ---

Description: Asphalt and concrete paving for parking lots, access roads, and loading areas. Temperature-sensitive installation (asphalt). Trades: equipment operators, general laborers.
Common Values: Asphalt, concrete paving, pervious concrete, pavers; base course, geotextile
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; HeavyBid
Procore Coverage: Medium — under sitework code; daily log notes capture paving activities
Benchmarking Use: Compare paving cost per SF by surface type
Notes: Daily log notes for paving activities. Temperature and weather dependencies.

Description: Concrete sidewalks, curbs, gutters, ADA ramps, and pavers. ADA compliance is a common observation and punch item trigger. Trades: concrete workers.
Common Values: Concrete sidewalks, curb and gutter, ADA detectable warning pavers
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — under sitework code; ADA observations and punch items
Benchmarking Use: Compare sidewalk cost per LF and ADA compliance defect rate
Notes: ADA compliance observations and punch items are a common signal.

Description: Trees, shrubs, ground cover, turf, and irrigation systems. Usually one of the last exterior scopes. Plant survival warranty (typically 1 year) extends beyond project completion. Trades: landscape contractors.
Common Values: Trees, shrubs, sod, seed, mulch, irrigation pipe, drip, spray heads, controllers
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — usually its own cost code; punch items for plant survival and irrigation
Benchmarking Use: Compare landscaping cost per SF of site area and plant replacement rates
Notes: Punch items for plant survival and irrigation coverage. Warranty extends beyond project completion.

Description: Parking lot and pathway lighting — poles, bollard lights, and site electrical. Coordinates with site electrical underground. Trades: electricians.
Common Values: Light poles, bollard fixtures, LED site lights, photocells
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; AGi32
Procore Coverage: Low — sometimes under electrical; sometimes under sitework
Benchmarking Use: Compare site lighting cost per parking space and fixture density
Notes: Sometimes under electrical code; sometimes under sitework — inconsistent categorization.

Description: Chain link, ornamental, and security fencing with gates and barriers. Small scope on most projects. Trades: fence contractors.
Common Values: Chain link, ornamental aluminum, steel picket, slide gates, swing gates
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — small scope; under sitework code
Benchmarking Use: Compare fencing cost per LF by type
Notes: Small scope. Usually under general sitework cost code.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from building element entities. Cross-domain composites do NOT go here. ---

Description: Total cost of each building system divided by gross building area (SF). The primary unit cost benchmark for comparing systems across projects. Calculation: system cost / gross SF. Source: budget line items, cost codes, building area.
Common Values: $5-$50/SF depending on system (structure $15-35, MEP $30-60, finishes $10-25)
Source Systems: P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Procore; MS Excel; RSMeans
Procore Coverage: Medium — depends on cost code granularity and financial tool adoption
Benchmarking Use: Core benchmark — compare system cost/SF across project types and regions
Notes: Not all customers have financial tools. Exclude projects without cost data from benchmarks.

Element: Punch Item Rate by Element Category
Description: Count of punch items per unit area (per 1000 SF) or per unit count (per door, per fixture) for each element category. Tracks closeout quality. Calculation: punch count / area or unit count. Source: punch items, building area.
Common Values: 5-50 items per 1000 SF depending on element; doors 0.5-2 per door; fixtures 0.3-1 per fixture
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; Fieldwire; BIM 360
Procore Coverage: High — punch items are the strongest field signal for element quality
Benchmarking Use: Compare punch item density across projects to identify quality outliers
Notes: Validated 18-category keyword clustering on punch item names. 0% null on name field.

Description: Count of RFIs per system per million dollars of contract value. Tracks design coordination complexity. Calculation: RFI count / (contract value / $1M). Source: RFIs, contract value.
Common Values: 10-100 RFIs per $1M depending on system; structural and MEP generate the most
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; BIM 360; Submittal Exchange
Procore Coverage: Medium — RFI predicted_topic enables system mapping
Benchmarking Use: Compare RFI density to identify systems with higher design uncertainty
Notes: ML-classified RFI topic taxonomy (176 topics) enables mapping to building systems without NLP.

Description: Count of quality/safety observations per unit area or per system. Tracks field issue frequency. Calculation: observation count / area. Source: observations, building area.
Common Values: 1-20 observations per 1000 SF; envelope and finishes generate the most
Source Systems: Procore; BIM 360; Fieldwire
Procore Coverage: Medium — keyword clustering enables element mapping
Benchmarking Use: Compare observation rates to identify high-risk elements by project type
Notes: 18-category keyword clustering validated. Category filter (Quality vs. Safety) segments the data.

Description: Average days from submittal creation to final approval for each system. Tracks procurement timeline performance. Calculation: avg(approval_date - created_date) by system. Source: submittals.
Common Values: 14-60 days typical; curtain wall, structural steel, elevators among longest
Source Systems: Procore; PlanGrid; Submittal Exchange; BIM 360
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals exist but system mapping requires spec section or keyword
Benchmarking Use: Compare submittal cycle times to identify procurement bottlenecks
Notes: Overdue submittals (718K records) are a signal for procurement delays.

Description: Count of coordination issues per system or trade per million dollars. Tracks MEP/architectural coordination complexity. Calculation: issue count / (contract value / $1M). Source: coordination issues, contract value.
Common Values: 5-30 issues per $1M; MEP-heavy buildings generate the most
Source Systems: Procore; BIM 360; Navisworks; Revit; Solibri
Procore Coverage: Medium — dedicated table exists with issue type classification
Benchmarking Use: Compare coordination issue density across projects and systems
Notes: MEP-heavy. Issue types (Clash
